mnemonics.title: COCOMO Modes - O.S.E.
mnemonics.content: O.S.E. stands for Organic, Semi-detached, Embedded - the three modes of COCOMO estimation. Think: 'Organic systems are Simple, Semi-detached are in-between, Embedded are complex.'

mnemonics.title: Estimation Techniques - A.D.E.P.T.
mnemonics.content: A.D.E.P.T. stands for Analogous, Delphi, Expert Judgment, Parametric, Three-Point estimation techniques. Usage: When starting a new project, use Analogous (past projects), then refine with Delphi (expert rounds), use Expert Judgment, apply Parametric models, and finalize with Three-Point (optimistic, pessimistic, most likely).

mnemonics.title: WBS Steps - B.E.D.F.P.
mnemonics.content: B.E.D.F.P. stands for Break down project, Estimate each work package, Define dependencies, Find critical path, Plan schedule with buffers. Usage: Follow this sequence when creating Work Breakdown Structure.

mnemonics.title: PERT Formula - E.O.T.M.L.
mnemonics.content: E.O.T.M.L. stands for Expected Time = (Optimistic + 4×Most Likely + Pessimistic) / 6. Usage: Calculate activity duration when you have three estimates (best case, worst case, most likely case).

mnemonics.title: Function Point Counting - C.I.L.T.F.
mnemonics.content: C.I.L.T.F. stands for Count Internal Logical Files, External Interface Files, External Inputs, External Outputs, External Inquiries. Usage: When counting function points for a software application.

acronyms.usage: Use to estimate effort (person-months) and schedule based on project size (KLOC) and mode (organic, semi-detached, embedded). Formula: Effort = a × (KLOC)^b × EM.
Decompose project into manageable work packages. Essential first step before estimation and scheduling.
Identify the longest path through project tasks to determine minimum project duration. Activities on critical path have zero float.
Use for projects with high uncertainty. Calculate expected time using three estimates (optimistic, pessimistic, most likely).
Measure software size based on user requirements. Count external inputs, outputs, inquiries, internal logical files, and external interface files. Language-independent.
Estimate project size based on use cases. Calculate: UAW (unadjusted actor weights) × UUW (unadjusted use case weights) × TCF (technical complexity factor) × ECF (environment factor).
Traditional size metric. Used with productivity rates (LOC/person-month) to estimate effort. Language-dependent.
Track project performance. Key metrics: PV (Planned Value), EV (Earned Value), AC (Actual Cost). SPI = EV/PV, CPI = EV/AC.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a chain with multiple links in parallel. The longest chain (critical path) determines how long the entire chain takes to complete. Any delay to a link on the longest chain delays the whole project.
Think of an inverted tree or organization chart. The root is the project (top), branches are phases/components (middle), and leaves are work packages (bottom). Each level is more detailed.

Picture a horizontal timeline with bars. Each bar represents a task. Bar length = task duration. Bars can overlap for parallel tasks or connect with arrows for dependencies. Milestones appear as diamonds.

Visualize five types of boxes: Inputs (data coming in), Outputs (reports/screens), Inquiries (queries), Internal files (data stored), and External interfaces (files shared). Count and weight each box type.
Picture three arrows: short (optimistic), long (pessimistic), medium (most likely). The expected time sits between them, weighted toward the most likely. Formula: (O + 4M + P) / 6.
Imagine stacking blocks (resources) in a container (project). If blocks overflow, redistribute them. Goal: smooth, non-overfilling arrangement without extending project duration.

analogies.analogy: Estimating software project effort is like estimating time to build a house. You consider: house size (function points/LOC), complexity of design (project mode), team experience (productivity rate), and tools available (development environment). A 1000 sq ft simple house takes less time than a 1000 sq ft complex mansion.

WBS is like planning a wedding. Break into major components: venue, catering, decoration, invitations, photography. Each component breaks into tasks: venue → select location, book date, negotiate price. Tasks break into subtasks. Never forget any detail.

Critical path is like the主线 (main storyline) in a movie. The movie cannot be completed until all main storyline scenes are filmed, even though side stories (non-critical tasks) can be filmed in parallel or delayed without affecting the release date.

Function Points measure software like a floor plan measures a building - by what it does (rooms, doors, windows). LOC measures like measuring by construction materials - bricks, cement, steel. Same building, different measurement perspectives.

Assigning developers to tasks is like assigning players to positions in a cricket team. You need the right person for each role (batsman, bowler, wicketkeeper). Overloading one player (single developer on multiple critical tasks) causes burnout and failure.

Adding buffers to project schedule is like keeping a spare tire in a car. You hope not to need it, but when unexpected problems occur (flat tire = critical bug), you're prepared. Without buffers, any unexpected event derails the entire schedule.
